questionText: The complexity class P consists of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in:
explanation: P is the class of decision problems solvable in time bounded by a polynomial in the input size on a deterministic Turing machine. These are considered 'efficiently solvable'.
distractorRationales: Exponential time defines EXP, a much larger class than P.
Constant time is far too restrictive to define P.
Logarithmic space defines L, a space class, not the time class P.

questionText: The class NP is best characterized as decision problems whose 'yes' instances have:
options: Certificates verifiable in polynomial time by a deterministic machine
Solutions findable in polynomial time deterministically
explanation: NP = problems for which a proposed solution (certificate/witness) can be VERIFIED in polynomial time. Equivalently, they are solvable in polynomial time by a nondeterministic Turing machine.
distractorRationales: Finding the solution deterministically in polynomial time is the definition of P, not NP (whether P = NP is open).
NP problems do have algorithms (e.g., brute force); the question is efficiency.
Input size is not what defines NP; verifiability of certificates is.

questionText: A problem is NP-complete if it is in NP and:
options: Every problem in NP reduces to it in polynomial time (it is NP-hard)
It is solvable in polynomial time
It is in P but not NP
explanation: An NP-complete problem is in NP and is NP-hard: every NP problem polynomial-time reduces to it. Thus it is among the 'hardest' problems in NP — if any one is in P, then P = NP.
distractorRationales: If an NP-complete problem were known to be in polynomial time, that would prove P = NP, which is unresolved.
NP-complete problems are decidable; undecidability is a separate notion.
Every NP-complete problem is in NP by definition; the phrasing 'in P but not NP' is contradictory.

questionText: Which problem was the first proven NP-complete (Cook-Levin theorem)?
explanation: The Cook-Levin theorem (1971) established that Boolean satisfiability (SAT) is NP-complete by directly simulating any polynomial-time nondeterministic Turing machine with a Boolean formula.
distractorRationales: Sorting is solvable in O(n log n), comfortably in P.
Shortest path (single-source, non-negative) is in P via Dijkstra's algorithm.
Minimum spanning tree is in P via Kruskal's or Prim's algorithm.

questionText: The relationship between P and NP that is currently known (proven) is:
explanation: It is proven that P ⊆ NP (any problem solvable deterministically in polynomial time can have its solution verified in polynomial time). Whether the inclusion is strict (P ≠ NP) is the famous open question.
distractorRationales: P = NP is unproven (and widely conjectured false); it is the open Millennium Prize problem.
P cannot properly contain NP; the containment goes the other way.
P and NP overlap (P ⊆ NP), so they are not disjoint.

questionText: To prove a new problem X is NP-complete, the standard two-step strategy is to show:
options: X ∈ NP, and reduce a known NP-complete problem to X in polynomial time
X ∈ P, and reduce X to SAT
X is undecidable, and reduce X to the halting problem
explanation: Membership in NP (verify certificates in poly time) plus a polynomial-time reduction FROM a known NP-complete problem TO X (establishing NP-hardness) together prove X is NP-complete.
distractorRationales: Showing X ∈ P would not prove NP-completeness; and you reduce a known NP-complete problem TO X, not X to SAT.
Undecidability and the halting problem concern computability, not NP-completeness.
Space requirements relate to PSPACE/EXPSPACE, not the NP-completeness proof method.

questionText: Which of the following is NOT known to be NP-complete?
explanation: Linear programming is solvable in polynomial time (e.g., interior-point or ellipsoid methods), so it is in P, not NP-complete. 3-SAT, Hamiltonian cycle, and vertex cover are all classic NP-complete problems.
distractorRationales: 3-SAT is the canonical NP-complete problem.
Hamiltonian cycle is a well-known NP-complete problem.
Vertex cover is a standard NP-complete problem.

questionText: A polynomial-time reduction from problem A to problem B (A ≤ₚ B) implies that:
options: If B is in P, then A is in P (and if A is hard, B is at least as hard)
A is always harder than B
explanation: A ≤ₚ B means instances of A can be transformed into instances of B in polynomial time. So a polynomial algorithm for B yields one for A — B is at least as hard as A.
distractorRationales: The reduction makes B at least as hard as A, not A harder than B.
The reduction precisely relates their difficulties; they are not unrelated.
Reductions among NP problems do not imply undecidability.

questionText: The class co-NP consists of:
options: Problems whose complements are in NP (NO instances have poly-time verifiable certificates)
explanation: co-NP is the class of decision problems whose complement is in NP — i.e., the 'no' answers have short, poly-time verifiable certificates. Whether NP = co-NP is open. (Tautology checking is a classic co-NP problem.)
distractorRationales: co-NP is a specific class defined via complements, not simply 'everything not in NP'.
Constant-time solvability is unrelated to co-NP.
co-NP equals P only if certain open conjectures hold; it is not known to equal P.

questionText: If a single NP-complete problem were shown to be solvable in polynomial time, the consequence would be:
options: P = NP, because all NP problems reduce to it in polynomial time
Only that one problem becomes easy; others stay hard
P would become a subset of co-NP only
explanation: Every NP problem reduces in polynomial time to any NP-complete problem. A polynomial algorithm for one NP-complete problem could solve every NP problem in polynomial time, proving P = NP.
distractorRationales: Because of completeness, solving one efficiently solves all of NP, not just that one.
It would make NP problems efficiently solvable, not undecidable.
It would collapse P and NP entirely, a far stronger consequence than a co-NP subset relation.

questionText: Which statement about the relationship between NP-hard and NP-complete is correct?
options: Every NP-complete problem is NP-hard, but some NP-hard problems are not in NP (so not NP-complete)
NP-hard problems are always in P
NP-complete is a strict superset of NP-hard
explanation: NP-hard means 'at least as hard as every NP problem' but need not be in NP (e.g., the halting problem is NP-hard but undecidable). NP-complete = NP-hard AND in NP. So NP-complete ⊆ NP-hard.
distractorRationales: An NP-hard problem must also be in NP to be NP-complete; not all NP-hard problems are in NP.
NP-hard problems are believed not to be in P (unless P = NP); some are even undecidable.
It is the reverse: NP-complete is a subset of NP-hard, not a superset.

questionText: The Cook-Levin theorem is significant because it shows:
options: SAT is NP-complete, providing the first 'anchor' from which other NP-completeness proofs follow by reduction
explanation: Cook-Levin established SAT as the first NP-complete problem by encoding any nondeterministic poly-time computation as a Boolean formula. This anchor lets one prove other problems NP-complete by reducing SAT (or known NP-complete problems) to them.
distractorRationales: It does not show SAT is in P; that would resolve P vs NP.
NP problems are decidable; the theorem is about completeness, not undecidability.
Polynomial reductions are central to the theorem, not shown nonexistent.

questionText: Which complexity relationship is known with certainty?
options: P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE ⊆ EXPTIME, with P ⊊ EXPTIME proven
explanation: The chain P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE ⊆ EXPTIME holds, and the time hierarchy theorem proves P ⊊ EXPTIME (strict). So at least one inclusion in the chain must be strict, though which ones are unknown.
distractorRationales: P = NP = PSPACE is not proven; these are major open questions.
NP = PSPACE is unproven (and conjectured false).
P ⊊ EXPTIME is proven, so P ≠ EXPTIME.

questionText: The time hierarchy theorem essentially states that:
options: Given more time, a deterministic machine can solve strictly more problems (e.g., DTIME(n) ⊊ DTIME(n³))
All time bounds give the same computational power
explanation: The time hierarchy theorem shows that with asymptotically more time (a 'time-constructible' increase), strictly more decision problems become solvable. This is what guarantees, for instance, P ⊊ EXPTIME.
distractorRationales: More time strictly increases power; bounds are not all equivalent.
Polynomial and exponential time differ (P ⊊ EXP), as the theorem implies.
The theorem is a proven mathematical result, not a statement about undecidability.

questionText: Which is a correct example of a problem in P (polynomial time)?
options: Determining whether a number is prime (AKS primality test)
explanation: Primality testing was shown to be in P by the AKS algorithm (2002). SAT, the TSP decision problem, and subset sum are all NP-complete.
distractorRationales: SAT is NP-complete, not known to be in P.
The TSP decision problem is NP-complete.
Subset sum is NP-complete.

questionText: A 'pseudo-polynomial' time algorithm (like the DP for subset sum, O(nS)) is NOT a true polynomial-time algorithm because:
options: Its runtime is polynomial in the numeric VALUE of the input (S), not in the input's bit-length (log S)
It only works on small inputs
explanation: Subset sum's O(nS) DP is polynomial in S, but S is given in binary using only log S bits. Relative to input size, the runtime is exponential, so it is pseudo-polynomial — subset sum remains NP-complete.
distractorRationales: The issue is the dependence on the value's magnitude, not memory usage.
Pseudo-polynomial algorithms are typically deterministic; randomness is unrelated.
It works on all inputs but is slow when numeric values are large relative to input length.

questionText: Reducing 3-SAT to the Independent Set problem demonstrates that:
options: Independent Set is NP-hard (and being in NP, NP-complete)
explanation: A polynomial reduction FROM the known NP-complete 3-SAT TO Independent Set shows Independent Set is at least as hard as 3-SAT, i.e., NP-hard. Since Independent Set is also in NP, it is NP-complete.
distractorRationales: The reduction says nothing about putting 3-SAT in P.
It shows Independent Set is NP-hard, evidence it is NOT in P (unless P = NP).
3-SAT is decidable; the reduction concerns NP-completeness, not undecidability.

questionText: A 'verifier' for a problem in NP, given input x and a certificate c, must:
options: Run in time polynomial in |x| and accept some certificate exactly when x is a yes-instance
Solve the problem from scratch without c
explanation: A polynomial-time verifier checks a candidate certificate c against input x; for yes-instances there exists a c that makes it accept (in poly time), and for no-instances no c works. This certificate-verification view defines NP.
distractorRationales: The verifier must run in polynomial, not exponential, time.
The certificate is essential; the verifier uses it to check the answer quickly.
The point of a verifier is to check a given certificate, not to solve the problem independently.

questionText: Which of the following best describes an 'intractable' problem in complexity theory?
options: A problem with no known polynomial-time algorithm (e.g., NP-hard problems, assuming P ≠ NP)
A problem solvable in O(n) time
A problem with a small input
explanation: Intractable problems are those lacking efficient (polynomial-time) algorithms. NP-hard problems are the standard examples, believed intractable under the widely held conjecture that P ≠ NP.
distractorRationales: An O(n) algorithm is efficient — the opposite of intractable.
Input size alone does not determine intractability.
Many decidable problems are tractable (in P); decidability does not imply intractability.

questionText: If P ≠ NP, then for NP-complete problems we know that:
options: No polynomial-time algorithm exists for them
They can be solved in logarithmic time
They are no longer in NP
explanation: If P ≠ NP, then by definition no NP-complete problem can be in P, so none has a polynomial-time algorithm. (They remain decidable and in NP regardless.)
distractorRationales: NP-complete problems are already decidable; P ≠ NP does not change that.
Logarithmic time is far below polynomial; this is impossible for NP-complete problems.
They remain in NP by definition; P ≠ NP does not remove them from NP.
